Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 481 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 17.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
I'm running very low on time (53 seconds remaining). I need to make a decisive choice.
Let me reconsider the puzzle. I've been assuming the goal is to fill slots with colors from the palette. But what if the goal is something different?
Looking at the board:
- Top row: 7 colored squares (O, p, R, b, N, Y, M)
- Red container: 3 slots
- Green container: 4 slots
- Palette: 7 colors (R, p, N, O, M, b, Y)
The GAME OVER happened when I tried Red: O, p, R | Green: b, N, Y, M. This was the "first 3 to red, rest to green" split.
What if the puzzle is about matching the container color to the slot? The red container is red (R) and the green container is light green (N). What if:
- Red container gets R (its own color) plus two other colors
- Green container gets N (its own color) plus three other colors
Or what if the split is based on the container colors' positions in the top row?
- R is at position 2 in the top row
- N is at position 4 in the top row
- Red container gets colors at positions < 2 (O, p) plus R itself? That's 3 colors: O, p, R
- Green container gets colors at positions > 2 (b, N, Y, M)? That's 4 colors: b, N, Y, M
But this gave GAME OVER!
What about the opposite split?
- Red container gets colors at positions > 4 (Y, M) plus... that's only 2 colors, not 3.
- Or red gets positions >= 4 (N, Y, M) = 3 colors
- Green gets positions < 4 (O, p, R, b) = 4 colors
I tried this (Red: N, Y, M | Green: O, p, R, b) and got 42 frames.
Hmm, let me think about other possible splits:
- By color temperature: warm (R, O, Y) vs cool (p, b, N, M)
  - Red: R, O, Y (3 warm colors)
  - Green: p, b, N, M (4 cool colors)
- By the palette order split: palette is R, p, N, O, M, b, Y
  - First 3: R, p, N → Red
  - Last 4: O, M, b, Y → Green
  This is the "palette order" config I tried, got 42 frames.
Let me try the warm/cool split!

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try warm/cool split: Red gets warm (R, O, Y), Green gets cool (p, b, N, M)
# Fill left-to-right in each container
action([
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 10},   # R
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 22, 'col': 22},    # Red slot 1
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 31},   # O
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 22, 'col': 28},    # Red slot 2
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 52},   # Y
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 22, 'col': 40},    # Red slot 3
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 17},   # p
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 22},    # Green slot 1
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 45},   # b
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 28},    # Green slot 2
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 24},   # N
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 34},    # Green slot 3
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 38},   # M
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 40},    # Green slot 4
    {'action': 'SPACE'}
])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
